title: Libraries + Error Debugging
subtitle: Use Superpowers & Fix Bugs Like a Pro!
overview: In this session, students discover how Python libraries supercharge their programs — from math to graphics to data. They also learn the art of debugging: reading error messages, understanding common error types, and fixing bugs step by step. No more fear of the red text!

images._note: Add photos from the actual session — error screenshots, whiteboard bug-hunt diagrams, library demos, activity shots.

keyTopics: Library — A collection of pre-written code you can reuse (like a toolbox!)
import — The keyword that loads a library into your program
pip install — The command to download and install third-party libraries
Standard Library — Libraries that come WITH Python: math, random, datetime, os
Third-Party Libraries — Libraries you install: numpy, pandas, matplotlib, requests
SyntaxError — Python can't read your code (wrong spelling/punctuation)
NameError — You used a variable or function that doesn't exist yet
TypeError — You mixed up data types (e.g. adding a number to a string)
IndexError — You asked for a list item at a position that doesn't exist
ZeroDivisionError — You tried to divide a number by zero
Traceback — The error report that tells you WHICH LINE caused the crash
try / except — Wrap risky code to catch errors gracefully instead of crashing

resources.title: PyPI — Python Package Index (Browse all libraries)

activity.title: Bug Hunt + Library Explorer
parts: Printed or on-screen buggy code sheets
Python standard libraries: math, random, datetime
steps: Part 1 — Library Explorer: `import math` and use math.sqrt(), math.pi, math.factorial()
Try `import random` — use random.randint(1, 10) to make a dice roller
Try `import datetime` — print today's date with datetime.date.today()
Part 2 — Bug Hunt: Open the buggy code sheet with 5 hidden errors
Read each Traceback message carefully — find the line number and error type
Fix each bug one by one: SyntaxError → NameError → TypeError → IndexError → ZeroDivisionError
Part 3 — Wrap a risky line in try/except and print a friendly error message instead of crashing
Challenge: Install an external library with pip (e.g. `pip install cowsay`) and use it!

options: A place where you borrow physical books
A collection of pre-written code you can import and reuse in your program
quiz.explanation: A library is a ready-made toolbox of code! Instead of writing everything from scratch, you import a library and use its functions instantly.

quiz.q: Which keyword is used to load a library into your Python program?
quiz.explanation: `import` is the magic word! `import math` loads the math library. `import random` loads the random library. One line = superpowers!

options: Downloads and installs the numpy library from the internet
quiz.explanation: `pip` is Python's package manager — like an app store for libraries! `pip install` downloads and installs any library from PyPI (Python Package Index).

quiz.q: Which of these is a STANDARD library — it comes built-in with Python?
quiz.explanation: `math` is part of Python's Standard Library — no installation needed! numpy, pandas, and matplotlib are third-party libraries you must install with pip.

quiz.q: What type of error does this code cause? `print('Hello' + 5)`
quiz.explanation: TypeError! You can't add a string ('Hello') and an integer (5) directly. Python doesn't know how to mix them. Fix: `print('Hello' + str(5))`

options: Python cannot understand your code due to wrong spelling or punctuation
You accessed a list item that doesn't exist
quiz.explanation: SyntaxError = Python can't read your code! Common causes: missing colon after if/for, unclosed bracket, or misspelled keyword. Like a grammar mistake in a sentence.

quiz.q: You write `print(score)` but never created the variable `score`. What error occurs?
quiz.explanation: NameError! Python says: 'I don't know what score is — you never told me!' Always define your variables before using them.

options: A way to go back in time in your code
Python's error report showing which line caused the crash and why
A type of loop that runs backwards
A command to undo the last action
quiz.explanation: Traceback is your BUG MAP! It shows the file name, line number, and error type. Always read the LAST line first — that's the actual error message.

quiz.q: What error occurs when you run `my_list = [1, 2, 3]` and then `print(my_list[10])`?
quiz.explanation: IndexError! The list only has items at positions 0, 1, 2. Asking for position 10 is like asking for page 500 in a 3-page book — it doesn't exist!
ZeroDivisionError! Dividing by zero is mathematically impossible. Python raises this error to stop the program instead of giving a wrong answer.

quiz.q: What does the `try` block do in a try/except statement?
options: It always skips the code inside
It contains the risky code that might cause an error
quiz.explanation: The `try` block wraps the risky code. Python ATTEMPTS to run it. If an error happens, it jumps to the `except` block instead of crashing!

options: Runs only when there is NO error
Runs when an error occurs in the try block, preventing a crash
quiz.explanation: `except` catches the error! Instead of crashing, your program runs the except block. You can print a friendly message like 'Oops! Please enter a valid number.'

quiz.q: Which library would you use to generate a RANDOM number in Python?
quiz.explanation: `random` library! Use `random.randint(1, 6)` to simulate a dice roll. It's part of Python's standard library — just `import random`!

quiz.q: What does `import math` followed by `math.sqrt(16)` return?
quiz.explanation: `math.sqrt(16)` returns 4.0! Square root of 16 is 4. The math library has tons of useful functions: sqrt, pow, pi, factorial, floor, ceil and more.

quiz.q: A student's code crashes with this message: `ZeroDivisionError: division by zero` on line 7. What should they check FIRST?
options: Line 7 where a variable is being divided — check if the denominator can be zero
The import statements at the top
quiz.explanation: Always go to the line number in the Traceback first! Line 7 has a division — check if the variable being divided by could ever equal zero, and add a check or try/except.
